metadata.title: Input-gradient space particle inference for neural network ensembles
organizations: Aalto University, Helsinki University, University of Manchester

sections.title: Repulsive deep ensembles (RDEs) [1]
content: Description:Train an ensemble using Wasserstein gradient descent, which employs a kernelized repulsion term to diversify the particles.
Thedriving forcedirects particles towards high density regions.
Therepulsion forcepushes particles away to enforce diversity.
Problem:Weight-space or function-space repulsion often results in underfitting or is ineffective.

figures.description: Particle update rule formula showing driving and repulsion forces

sections.title: Defining the input-gradient kernel
content: Given a base kernel, we define the kernel in the input-gradient space for a minibatch:
We choose the RBF kernel on a unit sphere:

figures.description: RBF kernel and gradient normalization formulas

sections.title: First-order repulsive deep ensembles (FoRDEs)
content: Possible advantages:
Each member is guaranteed to represent a different function.
Issues of weight- and function-space repulsion are avoided.
Encourages learning different features for robustness.

figures.description: FoRDE model architecture and training flow diagram

sections.title: Main takeaways
content: 1. Input-gradient-space repulsion can perform better than weight- and function-space.
2. Better corruption robustness can be achieved by configuring the repulsion kernel.

sections.title: Benchmark comparison
content: Table 1: FoRDE-PCA achieves the best performance under corruptions on CIFAR-100.

sections.title: Illustrative experiments
content: FoRDEs capture higher uncertainty than baselines in regions outside of the training data.

sections.title: Benchmark comparison (cont.)
content: Table 2: Results on CIFAR-10 dataset.
[1] F. D'Angelo and V. Fortuin, "Repulsive deep ensembles are Bayesian," NeurIPS 2021.[2] C. Liu et al., "Understanding and Accelerating Particle-Based Variational Inference," ICML 2019.

sections.title: Tuning the lengthscales Σ
content: Each lengthscale is inversely proportional to the strength of the repulsion force.
Proposition:Apply strong forces in high-variance dimensions and weak forces in low-variance dimensions.

figures.description: Lengthscale derivative formula for repulsion force
High-variance dimension diagram showing strong repulsion
Low-variance dimension diagram showing weak repulsion

sections.title: Lengthscale tuning experiments
content: Blue lines show accuracies of FoRDEs, dotted orange show Deep Ensembles.
FoRDEs exhibit small performance degradations on clean images but become more robust against natural corruptions.

figures.description: Accuracy vs Alpha plots for ResNet18 on CIFAR-100
